titlestmt.titleproper: Guide to the John Willis, Photographs of Dakota Access Pipeline Protests in North Dakota
Willis (John), Photographs of Dakota Access Pipeline Protests in North Dakota
titlestmt.author: by Matthew Daniel Mason

publicationstmt.publisher: Beinecke Rare Book and Manuscript Library

maintenanceagency.agencyname: Beinecke Rare Book and Manuscript Library

descriptivenote.p: Finding aid description and metadata in Archives at Yale are licensed under a CC0 1.0 Universal (CC0 1.0) Public Domain Dedication. This means that anyone may copy, modify, and distribute this descriptive metadata without restriction or permission. The CC0 license is explained in full on the Creative Commons website: CC0 1.0 Universal (CC0 1.0) Public Domain Dedication. More information is available on our Help page.

maintenanceevent.eventdescription: This finding aid was produced using ArchivesSpace on Thursday November 28, 2024 at 00:48

did.unittitle: Photographs of Dakota Access Pipeline Protests in North Dakota

corpname.part: Beinecke Rare Book and Manuscript Library

did.abstract: Photographs created by John Willis during six trips from September through December 2016 that document a resistance movement based near Cannon Ball, North Dakota, and eastern edge of the Standing Rock Indian Reservation protesting against the Energy Transfer Partners project to construct a petroleum pipeline known as the Dakota Access Pipeline (DAPL) at the site and through North Dakota and South Dakota as well as across Iowa into Illinois. The Standing Rock Sioux Tribe led the protest with support a grassroots resistance movement known as "Mní Wičóni" (Lakota for "water is life") and NoDAPL (No Dakota Access Pipeline). Many images also appear in John Willis, Mni Wiconi - Water Is Life Honoring the Water Protectors at Standing Rock and Everywhere in the Ongoing Struggle for Indigenous Sovereignty (Staunton, Virginia: George F. Thompson Publishing, 2019).

acqinfo.head: Immediate Source of Acquisition
acqinfo.p: Purchased from John Willis on the Walter McClintock Memorial Fund, 2017.

arrangement.head: Arrangement
arrangement.p: Organized into two series: I. Inkjet Prints, 13 x 19 inches (32.8 x 48.2 centimeters), 2016. Inkjet Prints, 24 x 31 inches (61 x 78.7 centimeters), 2016.

bioghist.head: John Willis (born 1957)
bioghist.p: John Willis is a documentary photographer and emeritus professor of photography at Marlboro College in Marlboro, Vermont. His monographs include Recycled Realities , co-authored with Tom Young (Center for American Places, 2002), Views from the Reservation (Center for American Places, 2010), and Mni Wiconi - Water Is Life Honoring the Water Protectors at Standing Rock and Everywhere in the Ongoing Struggle for Indigenous Sovereignty (Staunton, Virginia: George F. Thompson Publishing, 2019). Willis has photographs in the permanent collections of the Whitney Museum of American Art, the Museum of Fine Arts in Boston, and the Museum of Fine Arts in Houston, among others. He is also a co-founder with Bill Ledger of the In-Sight Photography Project, a photography outreach project to youth in Brattleboro, Vermont.

prefercite.head: Preferred Citation
prefercite.p: John Willis, Photographs of Dakota Access Pipeline Protests in North Dakota. Yale Collection of Western Americana, Beinecke Rare Book and Manuscript Library.

processinfo.head: Processing Information
processinfo.p: Collections are processed to a variety of levels, depending on the work necessary to make them usable, their perceived research value, the availability of staff, competing priorities, and whether or not further accruals are expected. The library attempts to provide a basic level of preservation and access for all collections, and does more extensive processing of higher priority collections as time and resources permit. These materials have been arranged and described according to national and local standards. For more information, please refer to the Beinecke Manuscript Unit Processing Manual.
Many discrete images share the same title throughout the collection.
Titled, signed, and dated by the photographer on versos of prints.

Titles in this guide are from the inscriptions by the photographer on versos of prints. Variant titles from Mni Wiconi - Water Is Life Honoring the Water Protectors at Standing Rock and Everywhere in the Ongoing Struggle for Indigenous Sovereignty (Staunton, Virginia: George F. Thompson Publishing, 2019) as well as from Willis's website, jwillis.net, accessed in April 2021.
Each folder in the collection contains a single inkjet print.

scopecontent.head: Description of Photographs
scopecontent.p: Photographs created by John Willis during six trips from September through December 2016 that document a resistance movement based near Cannon Ball, North Dakota, and eastern edge of the Standing Rock Indian Reservation protesting the Energy Transfer Partners project to construct a petroleum pipeline known as the Dakota Access Pipeline (DAPL) at the site and through North Dakota and South Dakota as well as across Iowa into Illinois. The Standing Rock Sioux Tribe led the protest with support a grassroots resistance movement known as "Mní Wičóni" (Lakota for "water is life") and NoDAPL (No Dakota Access Pipeline). Many images also appear in John Willis, Mni Wiconi - Water Is Life Honoring the Water Protectors at Standing Rock and Everywhere in the Ongoing Struggle for Indigenous Sovereignty (Staunton, Virginia: George F. Thompson Publishing, 2019).

Images printed as 224 inkjet color prints document spiritual ceremonies related to the water as well as resistance actions at the proposed drilling site as well as at Bismarck and Mandan in North Dakota. Additionally, many images document temporary camps established by protestors, chiefly the Oceti Sakowin camp (Očhéthi Šakówiŋ is Dakota for "Seven Council Fires" or peoples known by the exonym of Sioux), as well as portraits of activists.

Individual prints are 32.8 x 48.2 centimeters and 61 x 78.7 centimeters. Titled and signed and dated by the photographer on versos of prints. Dates indicate the time of image capture rather than physical creation of the print.

Titles in this guide are from the inscriptions by the photographer on versos of prints. Variant titles from Mni Wiconi - Water Is Life Honoring the Water Protectors at Standing Rock and Everywhere in the Ongoing Struggle for Indigenous Sovereignty (Staunton, Virginia: George F. Thompson Publishing, 2019) as well as from Willis' website, jwillis.net, accessed in April 2021.

accessrestrict.head: Conditions Governing Access
accessrestrict.p: The John Willis, Photographs of Dakota Access Pipeline Protests in North Dakota are the physical property of the Beinecke Rare Book and Manuscript Library, Yale University. Literary rights, including copyright, belong to the authors or their legal heirs and assigns. For further information, consult the appropriate curator.

geogname.part: Standing Rock Indian Reservation (N.D. and S.D.)
Standing Rock Sioux Tribe of North & South Dakota

did.unittitle: Inkjet Prints, 13 x 19 inches (32.8 x 48.2 centimeters)

arrangement.head: Arrangement
arrangement.p: Series I is organized into four subseries: Ceremonies, Resistance Actions, Camps, and Portraits.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Many of the prints in this series duplicate images in Series II.

arrangement.head: Arrangement
arrangement.p: Organized under two headings: Returning the Stone and Water Ceremony.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Photographs related to spiritual ceremonies in North Dakota led and attended by activists opposed to the Energy Transfer Partners project to construct a petroleum pipeline known as the Dakota Access Pipeline (DAPL).

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Photographs that document a prayer circle of activists opposed to the Energy Transfer Partners project to construct a petroleum pipeline known as the Dakota Access Pipeline (DAPL). During the ceremony led by Lakota women Marie Brown and Roxanne Thompson from the Standing Rock Reservation, Ms. Brown returning a sacred stone to the Missouri River near the drilling site of the Dakota Access Pipeline in North Dakota in November 2016.

Title of this subseries from an essay by Willis in John Willis, Mni Wiconi - Water Is Life Honoring the Water Protectors at Standing Rock and Everywhere in the Ongoing Struggle for Indigenous Sovereignty (Staunton, Virginia: George F. Thompson Publishing, 2019), 132.

arrangement.head: Arrangement
arrangement.p: Arranged in likely sequence of the event.

title.part: Building a Bridge from Dead Trees to Help Grandmother Pass Mud to Reach Cannonball River
Building the Bridge Across the Mud
Building the Bridge
Prayer Circle for Grandmother and Daughter Before They do Prayer Ceremony in Cannonball River, Cold November Weather
Circle Prayer in Support of Grandmother and Daughter Wanting to do Prayers in Cannonball River
Grandmother and Daughter Returning Sacred Stone in Prayer to Mní Wičóni
Grandmother and Daughter Enter Cannonball River to Pray
Grandmother and Daughter After Making Prayers in Cannonball River
Lakota Grandmother and Daughter After Praying in the Cannonball River on a November Day
Lakota Grandmother and Daughter After Praying in the Cannonball River on a November Day
Lakota Grandmother and Daughter After Praying in the Cannonball River on a November Day
Returning the Stone to the River Prayer
Lakota Grandmother and Daughter After Prayers in the Cannonball River
Lakota Grandmother and Daughter After Making Prayers in the Cannonball River
Returning the Stone to the River Prayer

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Photographs that document a daily water ceremony at the Oceti Sakowin camp where activists gathered to protest the Energy Transfer Partners project to construct a petroleum pipeline known as the Dakota Access Pipeline (DAPL) near Cannon Ball, North Dakota, in September-December 2016. Images include views of the ceremony site and activities surrounding one of the observances.

Title of this subseries from an essay by Pearl Alhameed and Mary Lyons as told to Irene Chacon-Fredericks in John Willis, Mni Wiconi - Water Is Life Honoring the Water Protectors at Standing Rock and Everywhere in the Ongoing Struggle for Indigenous Sovereignty (Staunton, Virginia: George F. Thompson Publishing, 2019), 138-139.

arrangement.head: Arrangement
arrangement.p: Organized under two headings: Site and Activities.

title.part: Site of Women's Daily Water Ceremony, Cannonball River

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Also known as Site of the Women's Daily Water-Prayer Ceremony Held Along the Cannonball River, a Tributary of the Missouri River that Forms the Northern Border of Sioux County and the Standing Rock Indian Reservation and Site of the Daily Women's Prayer Ceremony for Mní Wičóni (Sacred Water is Life) Cannonball River

relatedmaterial.head: Related Materials
relatedmaterial.p: See also: Inkjet Prints, 24 x 31 inches (61 x 78.7 centimeters). Site of the Daily Women's Prayer Ceremony for Mní Wičóni (Sacred Water is Life) Cannonball River , 14 (Broadside)

title.part: Prayer Ties, Cannonball River's Edge

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Also known as Tobacco Prayer Ties, Cannonball River and Lakota Tobacco Prayer Ties Along the Cannonball River in the Morning Mist

relatedmaterial.head: Related Materials
relatedmaterial.p: See also: Inkjet Prints, 24 x 31 inches (61 x 78.7 centimeters). Tobacco Prayer Ties, Cannonball River , 15 (Broadside)

title.part: Indigenous Women Who Led Water Prayer March and Convinced Police to Pray Together

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Also known as Women Indigenous Led Action, They Told the Police They Would Peacefully Leave if the Police Participated in a Water Prayer, which the Police Did

relatedmaterial.head: Related Materials
relatedmaterial.p: See also: Inkjet Prints, 24 x 31 inches (61 x 78.7 centimeters). Women Indigenous Led Action, They Told the Police They Would Peacefully Leave if the Police Participated in a Water Prayer, which the Police Did , 16 (Broadside)

title.part: Morning Prayer Walk
Native Elder Leading Women's Morning Water Prayer Ceremony

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Woman in center of image is Mary Lyons, an Ojibwe elder

title.part: Woman Pouring Water Prayer into the Cannonball River
Women's Morning Prayer Walk to Cannonball River for Water Ceremony
Women's Morning Water Prayer Ceremony

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Woman in center of image is Mary Lyons, an Ojibwe elder

title.part: Women's Morning Water Prayer Ceremony
Women's Morning Water Prayer Ceremony
Women's Morning Water Prayer Ceremony, Cannonball River

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Photographs that document actions to protest the Energy Transfer Partners project to construct a petroleum pipeline known as the Dakota Access Pipeline (DAPL) in south central North Dakota and related images, September-December 2016.

arrangement.head: Arrangement
arrangement.p: Organized under seven headings: Backwater Sunday, November 20, 2016, Standing Rock; Bismarck, North Dakota; Mandan, North Dakota; North Dakota Highway 1806, Backwater Bridge, and Vicinity; Red Fawn Fallis Action; Turtle Island Action; and United States Military Veterans.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Photographs that document a standoff between members of the grassroots resistance movement opposing the Energy Transfer Partners project to construct a petroleum pipeline known as the Dakota Access Pipeline (DAPL) and local authorities including the Morton County Sheriff Department, the Mandan Fire Department, the Stutsman County Sheriff Department, and others at the Backwater Bridge on North Dakota Highway 1806 near Cannon Ball, North Dakota, during the evening of November 20, 2016. Images document the conflict itself as well as a tear gas victim and portraits of protestors during the event.

Title of this subseries from a poem by Gurudarhm Kalsa in John Willis, Mni Wiconi - Water Is Life Honoring the Water Protectors at Standing Rock and Everywhere in the Ongoing Struggle for Indigenous Sovereignty (Staunton, Virginia: George F. Thompson Publishing, 2019), 154.

arrangement.head: Arrangement
arrangement.p: Organized under three headings: Conflict Images, Tear Gas Victim, and Portraits of Protestors

title.part: Avoiding the Reach of the Water Cannons, Tear Gas, and Rubber Bullets, Backwater Bridge Standoff
Backwater Bridge Standoff

scopecontent.p: Also known as Unarmed Water Protectors Stand Up to Police Assault, Backwater Bridge

relatedmaterial.p: See also: Inkjet Prints, 24 x 31 inches (61 x 78.7 centimeters). Unarmed Water Protectors Stand Up to Police Assault, Backwater Bridge , 19 (Broadside)

Backwater Bridge Standoff
Backwater Bridge Standoff
Watching the Police Use Water Cannons, Tear Gas and More on Water Protectors the Night of November 20th
Watching the Standoff Unfold, Backwater Bridge

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Also known as Watching the Tear Gas, Rubber Bullet, Water Cannon Use and More, Backwater Bridge Standoff

relatedmaterial.head: Related Materials
relatedmaterial.p: See also: Inkjet Prints, 24 x 31 inches (61 x 78.7 centimeters). Watching the Tear Gas, Rubber Bullet, Water Cannon Use and More, Backwater Bridge Standoff , 21 (Broadside)

title.part: Witnessing Tear Gassing, Backwater Bridge Standoff

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Photographs that document actions at Bismarck, North Dakota, in September-December 2016, to protest the Energy Transfer Partners project to construct a petroleum pipeline known as the Dakota Access Pipeline (DAPL). Images include views of protestors as well as counter protestors and law enforcement at the actions.

title.part: Bismarck Rally Against Dakota Access Pipeline
Bismarck State Capital Action
Bismarck State Capital Action
Bismarck Street Action Drawing Attention to Cause
Bismarck, North Dakota, State Capital Action

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Also known as A Peaceful Protest Rally Against Locating Dakota Access Pipeline on Sovereign Lakota Land was Held on the North Dakota Statehouse Lawn in Bismarck

title.part: Drawing Attention to the Cause, Bismarck, North Dakota, City Limits, Residential Neighborhood

Ex-Police Officer from Wisconsin Who Left the Force Due to Injustice with treatment of Resistance Movements like Black Lives Matter. She then Came and Joined Mní Wičóni, No Dakota Access Pipeline Movement, Bismarck, North Dakota
Non-Native Volunteers to be Arrested, Bismarck Federal Building

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Also known as Non-Native Allies, Water Protectors, Surround Natives to Protect them from Arrest, Outside Federal Building, Bismarck, North Dakota, and Non-Native Allies Circling Native Water Protectors to Protect Them from Arrest During a Street Action that Stopped Traffic Outside the Federal Building in Bismarck, North Dakota, Where Camp Leaders and Legal Defense Representatives were Attempting to Learn the Status of Arrested Water Protectors #1

relatedmaterial.head: Related Materials
relatedmaterial.p: See also: Inkjet Prints, 24 x 31 inches (61 x 78.7 centimeters). Non-Native Allies, Water Protectors, Surround Natives to Protect them from Arrest, Outside Federal Building, Bismarck, North Dakota , 22 (Broadside)

Outside Federal Building, Bismarck, North Dakota
Outside Federal Building, Bismarck, North Dakota
Outside Federal Building, Bismarck, North Dakota

scopecontent.p: Also known as Standoff Outside the United States Courthouse in Bismarck

relatedmaterial.head: Related Materials
relatedmaterial.p: See also: Inkjet Prints, 24 x 31 inches (61 x 78.7 centimeters). Outside Federal Building, Bismarck, North Dakota , 24 (Broadside)

title.part: Pro Dakota Access Pipeline Protestors, Bismarck, North Dakota, Outside of Army Corps of Engineers Headquarters
Pro Dakota Access Pipeline Rally, Bismarck, North Dakota
Rally at Army Corps of Engineers Headquarters, Bismarck, North Dakota

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Also known as Water Protectors Protested at the Headquarters of the United States Army Corps of Engineers in Bismarck

title.part: Water Protector Arrested in Bismarck, North Dakota, for Stepping Across Imaginary Line
Water Protector Arrested in Bismarck, North Dakota, for Stepping Across Police Line to Pick a Flower After Being Told to Stay on Other Side of Drawn Line
Water Protectors, Federal Building, Bismarck, North Dakota

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Photographs that document actions at Mandan, North Dakota, in September-December 2016, to protest the Energy Transfer Partners project to construct a petroleum pipeline known as the Dakota Access Pipeline (DAPL), including the day of a hearing at the McLean County Memorial Courthouse for journalist Amy Goodman on October 17, 2016. Images include views of protestors and law enforcement at the actions.

title.part: Church Across from Mandan, North Dakota, Courthouse
Mandan Courthouse

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Also known as Pleading with Police to Consider Importance of Caring for Clean Water for Future Generations of All People, Mandan, North Dakota , and Pleading with Police Officers in Mandan to Consider Water for the Health of their Children, Grandchildren, and All Future Generations

relatedmaterial.head: Related Materials
relatedmaterial.p: See also: Inkjet Prints, 24 x 31 inches (61 x 78.7 centimeters). Pleading with Police to Consider Importance of Caring for Clean Water for Future Generations of All People, Mandan, North Dakota , 25 (Broadside)

title.part: Mandan Courthouse Day, Amy Goodman of Democracy Now was to be Charged
Mandan Courthouse Day, Day of Amy Goodman's Case

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Also known as Protestors at the Mandan County Memorial Courthouse in Mandan Attempt to Learn the Whereabouts and Charges Brought Against those Water Protectors who were Arrested

title.part: Mandan, North Dakota, Action by Energy Transfer Worksite

did.unittitle: North Dakota Highway 1806, Backwater Bridge, and Vicinity

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Photographs that document actions along North Dakota Highway 1806, also known as ND 1806 and Route 1806, as well as other sites near it in Morton County, September-December 2016, to protest the Energy Transfer Partners project to construct a petroleum pipeline known as the Dakota Access Pipeline (DAPL). The Backwater Bridge on North Dakota Highway 1806 served as the site of conflict between protesters and law enforcement. Images include views of protestors as well as law enforcement at the actions.

arrangement.head: Arrangement
arrangement.p: Organized under three headings: North Dakota Highway 1806, Backwater Bridge, and Vicinity.

title.part: American Indian Movement Roadblock, Route 1806
Martin Luther King Jr. Quoted, Route 1806
Red Warrior Horsemen Lead Way to North Camp, Route 1806 from Oceti Sakowin Camp

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Also known as Movement from Oceti Sokowin Camp to the North Camp along ND 1806 (A Section of the Lewis and Clark Trail)

title.part: Roadblock Designed to Stop Police from Raiding the Camp
Roadblock Route 1806 to Stop Police from Raiding Oceti Sakowin Camp
Route 1806 Roadblock
Route 1806 Roadblock Built by Water Protectors to Stop Police Raiding Camp, Taken Down Upon Elders' Request
Backwater Bridge, Personal Water Prayer While Receiving Threats from Riot Police
Water Prayer While Being Threatened by Riot Police, Backwater Bridge

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Also known as Praying in Defiance on the Backwater Bridge

title.part: Water Protector Defying Police on Backwater Bridge
Border Patrol Brought Down by North Dakota Governor to Assist with Resistance to Dakota Access Pipeline
Bullet Casings Fired by Energy Transfer Truck Driver After Pistol Whipping a Female Water Protector
Bullet Casings Shot by Energy Transfer Truck Driver After Pistol Whipping Female Water Protector
Dakota Access Pipeline Beaded Across Landscape

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Also known as Pipeline Sections Appear Like Beadwork Across the Landscape

Road to Mandan, North Dakota
Roadside Action

title.part: Roadside Action Near Mandan, North Dakota
Slowing Down Dakota Access Pipeline Construction One Day at a Time
Water Protector Who was Shot in the Eye Later on November 20 Blackwater Bridge Stand Off

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Woman identified as Vanessa Dundon

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Also known as Protecting the North Camp

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Photographs that document an action to support Red Fawn Fallis, a citizen of the Oglala Sioux Tribe and a Water Protector arrested on October 27, 2016, during an action protesting the Energy Transfer Partners project to construct a petroleum pipeline known as the Dakota Access Pipeline (DAPL).

title.part: Action to Free Red Fawn, Jailed Water Protector
Gathering to Call for Freeing Red Fawn, the Jailed Water Protector

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Photographs that document an action to occupy Turtle Island in the Cannonball River on Thanksgiving Day, November 24, 2016, to protest the Energy Transfer Partners project to construct a petroleum pipeline known as the Dakota Access Pipeline (DAPL). Includes images related to local authorities occupying the ridge of the island.

title.part: Thanksgiving Day, Turtle Island

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Also known as Turtle Island, Thanksgiving Day

relatedmaterial.p: See also: Inkjet Prints, 24 x 31 inches (61 x 78.7 centimeters). Turtle Island Action, Thanksgiving Day , 29 (Broadside)

title.part: Water Protector, Thanksgiving Action, Turtle Island, North Dakota
Water Protector, Turtle Island, Thanksgiving Day

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Photographs that document actions that included United States Military Veterans at North Dakota, September-December 2016, to protest the Energy Transfer Partners project to construct a petroleum pipeline known as the Dakota Access Pipeline (DAPL).

title.part: A Veteran's Funeral Procession
Veterans' March, Backwater Bridge
Veterans' Spreading of his Ashes and Memorial
Veterans Stand for Water Protectors, Back Water Bridge During Blizzard
Veterans Stand for Water Protectors, Back Water Bridge During Blizzard

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Also known as Veterans' Action on Backwater Bridge in Defense of Water Protectors #1

title.part: Veterans, Backwater Bridge

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Also known as Veterans Who Came to Stand for Water Protectors, Safeguarding them from Sheriff's Forces and Veterans' Action on Backwater Bridge in Defense of Water Protectors #2

relatedmaterial.head: Related Materials
relatedmaterial.p: See also: Inkjet Prints, 24 x 31 inches (61 x 78.7 centimeters). Veterans Who Came to Stand for Water Protectors, Safeguarding them from Sheriff's Forces , 30 (Broadside)

arrangement.head: Arrangement
arrangement.p: Organized under five headings: Camp Overviews, Camps at Dawn, Camps During and After a Blizzard, North Camp, and Camp Resources.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Photographs that document temporary camps, chiefly the Oceti Sakowin camp near Cannon Ball, North Dakota, and eastern edge of the Standing Rock Indian Reservation, established by activists protesting the Energy Transfer Partners project to construct a petroleum pipeline known as the Dakota Access Pipeline (DAPL), September-December 2016. Images include overviews of the camps, including views created at dawn or during and after a blizzard, as well as related to resources provided to inhabitants of the camps.

title.part: All Kinds of Resistance Groups Sent Support for this Indigenous-Led Movement
By the Front Gate, Oceti Sakowin
Camp Road Sign

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Also known as Oceti Sakowin Camp Road Sign

relatedmaterial.p: See also: Inkjet Prints, 24 x 31 inches (61 x 78.7 centimeters). Oceti Sakowin Camp Road Sign , 32 (Broadside)

"It's a Movement of Peaceful Resistance"

title.part: Sign for Neighbor's Buffalo Ranch
Sunset as Weather Changes
Tyvek Teepee Designed in Seattle, Man on Top is Maker Who Brought 30 to Donate

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Also known as After Successfully Crowdsourcing Funding, the Creator of Tyvek Tipis and Others from Seattle Designed, Built, and Delivered Thirty Tipis with Wood Stoves to Oceti Sakowin Camp

title.part: Outside a Camp Kitchen
Sitting Bull Quote
Winter Set In

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Also known as Camp Oceti Sakowin, First Blizzard and Jeep in Blizzard, Standing Rock

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Photographs related to North Camp, an encampment established adjacent to the Missouri River in late October 2016, by activists on property owned by Energy Transfer Partners in southern Morton County next to North Dakota Highway 1806 as well as a drilling site for its project to construct a petroleum pipeline known as the Dakota Access Pipeline (DAPL). The site was declared as eminent domain by the Cheyenne River Sioux Tribe citing its treaty rights on behalf of the activists. Images also document the aftermath of a raid on October 27, 2016, by local authorities and a private security firm.

title.part: Cheyenne River Tribe Leads Creation of North Camp by Claiming Rights Due to Imminent Domain Treaty Right
Moving into Setup North Camp
North Camp Belongings Returned After Police Raid When 100 were Arrested

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Photographs that document resources at camps for activists protesting the Energy Transfer Partners project to construct a petroleum pipeline known as the Dakota Access Pipeline (DAPL).

arrangement.head: Arrangement
arrangement.p: Organized under four headings: Art Tent, Community Kitchen, Medical Services, and Resource Distribution.

scopecontent.p: Also known as Painting Signs and Banners for Peaceful Protests to Come

Painting Silkscreen Banners

Silkscreen Shop

scopecontent.p: Portraits of activists protesting the Energy Transfer Partners project to construct a petroleum pipeline known as the Dakota Access Pipeline (DAPL), including at camps and at protest actions.

Lakota Unci (Grandmother) and her Shunka (Dog) Companion, She Lived Many Months in her Car

scopecontent.p: Also known as A Lakota Water Protector Who Gave Up Everything to Live in Camp

title.part: Water Protector

title.part: Water Protectors

did.unittitle: Inkjet Prints, 24 x 31 inches (61 x 78.7 centimeters)

arrangement.head: Arrangement
arrangement.p: Series II is organized into three subseries: Ceremonies, Resistance Actions, and Camps.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Most of the prints in this series duplicate images in Series I. The arrangement of prints in this series mirrors the arrangement of prints in Series I.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Photographs related to spiritual ceremonies in North Dakota led and attended by activists opposed to the Energy Transfer Partners project to construct a petroleum pipeline known as the Dakota Access Pipeline (DAPL).

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Photographs that document a daily water ceremony at the Oceti Sakowin camp where activists gathered to protest the Energy Transfer Partners project to construct a petroleum pipeline known as the Dakota Access Pipeline (DAPL) near Cannon Ball, North Dakota, in September-December 2016. Images include views of the ceremony site and activities surrounding one of the observances.

Title of this subseries from an essay by Pearl Alhameed and Mary Lyons as told to Irene Chacon-Fredericks in John Willis, Mni Wiconi - Water Is Life Honoring the Water Protectors at Standing Rock and Everywhere in the Ongoing Struggle for Indigenous Sovereignty (Staunton, Virginia: George F. Thompson Publishing, 2019), 138-139.

arrangement.head: Arrangement
arrangement.p: Organized under two headings: Site and Activities.

title.part: Site of the Daily Women's Prayer Ceremony for Mní Wičóni (Sacred Water is Life) Cannonball River

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Also known as Site of Women's Daily Water Ceremony, Cannonball River and Site of Women's Daily Water Ceremony, Cannonball River and Site of the Women's Daily Water-Prayer Ceremony Held Along the Cannonball River, a Tributary of the Missouri River that Forms the Northern Border of Sioux County and the Standing Rock Indian Reservation

relatedmaterial.head: Related Materials
relatedmaterial.p: See also: Inkjet Prints, 13 x 19 inches (32.8 x 48.2 centimeters). Site of Women's Daily Water Ceremony, Cannonball River , Box 2, folder 16

title.part: Tobacco Prayer Ties, Cannonball River

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Also known as Prayer Ties, Cannonball River's Edge and Lakota Tobacco Prayer Ties Along the Cannonball River in the Morning Mist

relatedmaterial.head: Related Materials
relatedmaterial.p: See also: Inkjet Prints, 13 x 19 inches (32.8 x 48.2 centimeters). Prayer Ties, Cannonball River's Edge , Box 2, folder 17

title.part: Women Indigenous Led Action, They Told the Police They Would Peacefully Leave if the Police Participated in a Water Prayer, which the Police Did

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Also known as Indigenous Women Who Led Water Prayer March and Convinced Police to Pray Together

relatedmaterial.head: Related Materials
relatedmaterial.p: See also: Inkjet Prints, 13 x 19 inches (32.8 x 48.2 centimeters). Indigenous Women Who Led Water Prayer March and Convinced Police to Pray Together , Box 2, folder 18

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Photographs that document actions to protest the Energy Transfer Partners project to construct a petroleum pipeline known as the Dakota Access Pipeline (DAPL) in south central North Dakota and related images, September-December 2016.

arrangement.head: Arrangement
arrangement.p: Organized under six headings: Backwater Sunday, November 20, 2016, Standing Rock; Bismarck, North Dakota; Mandan, North Dakota; North Dakota Highway 1806, Backwater Bridge, and Vicinity; Turtle Island Action; and United States Military Veterans

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Photographs that document a standoff between members of the grassroots resistance movement opposing the Energy Transfer Partners project to construct a petroleum pipeline known as the Dakota Access Pipeline (DAPL) and local authorities including the Morton County Sheriff Department, the Mandan Fire Department, the Stutsman County Sheriff Department, and others at the Backwater Bridge on North Dakota Highway 1806 near Cannon Ball, North Dakota, during the evening of November 20, 2016. Images document the conflict itself as well as a tear gas victim and portraits of protestors during the event.

Title of this subseries from a poem by Gurudarhm Kalsa in John Willis, Mni Wiconi - Water Is Life Honoring the Water Protectors at Standing Rock and Everywhere in the Ongoing Struggle for Indigenous Sovereignty (Staunton, Virginia: George F. Thompson Publishing, 2019), 154.

arrangement.head: Arrangement
arrangement.p: Organized under three headings: Conflict Images, Tear Gas Victim, and Portraits of Protestors

title.part: Running Out of Reach from Tear Gas, Water Cannons, Rubber Bullets, Etc., Backwater Bridge Standoff

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Also known as Retreating from Tear Gas, Backwater Bridge Standoff , Running from Tear Gas, Rubber Bullets, Water Cannons, Etc. , and Running from Tear Gas and Rubber Bullets

relatedmaterial.head: Related Materials
relatedmaterial.p: See also: Inkjet Prints, 13 x 19 inches (32.8 x 48.2 centimeters). Retreating from Tear Gas, Backwater Bridge Standoff , Box 3, folder 31

title.part: Throwing Tear Gas Back at Police, Backwater Bridge Standoff

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Also known as Throwing Back Tear Gas Towards the Police

relatedmaterial.head: Related Materials
relatedmaterial.p: See also: Inkjet Prints, 13 x 19 inches (32.8 x 48.2 centimeters). Throwing Tear Gas Back at Police, Backwater Bridge Standoff , Box 3, folder 32

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Also known as Backwater Bridge Standoff

relatedmaterial.head: Related Materials
relatedmaterial.p: See also: Inkjet Prints, 13 x 19 inches (32.8 x 48.2 centimeters). Backwater Bridge Standoff , Box 3, folder 28

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Also known as Tear Gas Victim Being Treated, Backwater Bridge Standoff and A Medic Treats a Tear-Gas Victim During the Conflict at Backwater Bridge

relatedmaterial.head: Related Materials
relatedmaterial.p: See also: Inkjet Prints, 13 x 19 inches (32.8 x 48.2 centimeters). Tear Gas Victim Being Treated, Backwater Bridge Standoff , Box 3, folder 36

title.part: Watching the Tear Gas, Rubber Bullet, Water Cannon Use and More, Backwater Bridge Standoff

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Also known as Watching the Standoff Unfold, Backwater Bridge

relatedmaterial.head: Related Materials
relatedmaterial.p: See also: Inkjet Prints, 13 x 19 inches (32.8 x 48.2 centimeters). Watching the Standoff Unfold, Backwater Bridge , Box 3, folder 41

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Photographs that document actions at Bismarck, North Dakota, in September-December 2016, to protest the Energy Transfer Partners project to construct a petroleum pipeline known as the Dakota Access Pipeline (DAPL). Images include views of protestors as well as counter protestors and law enforcement at the actions.

title.part: Non-Native Allies, Water Protectors, Surround Natives to Protect them from Arrest, Outside Federal Building, Bismarck, North Dakota

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Also known as Non-Native Volunteers to be Arrested, Bismarck Federal Building and Non-Native Allies Circling Native Water Protectors to Protect Them from Arrest During a Street Action that Stopped Traffic Outside the Federal Building in Bismarck, North Dakota, Where Camp Leaders and Legal Defense Representatives were Attempting to Learn the Status of Arrested Water Protectors #1

relatedmaterial.head: Related Materials
relatedmaterial.p: See also: Inkjet Prints, 13 x 19 inches (32.8 x 48.2 centimeters). Non-Native Volunteers to be Arrested, Bismarck Federal Building , Box 4, folder 50

title.part: Non-Native Allies, Water Protectors, Surround Natives to Protect them from Police, Outside Federal Building, Bismarck, North Dakota
Outside Federal Building, Bismarck, North Dakota

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Also known as Standoff Outside the United States Courthouse in Bismarck

relatedmaterial.head: Related Materials
relatedmaterial.p: See also: Inkjet Prints, 13 x 19 inches (32.8 x 48.2 centimeters). Outside Federal Building, Bismarck, North Dakota , Box 5, folder 58

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Photograph that documents an action at Mandan, North Dakota, in September-December 2016, to protest the Energy Transfer Partners project to construct a petroleum pipeline known as the Dakota Access Pipeline (DAPL). The image documents a view of a protestor and law enforcement at the action.

title.part: Pleading with Police to Consider Importance of Caring for Clean Water for Future Generations of All People, Mandan, North Dakota

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Also known as Mandan Courthouse and Pleading with Police Officers in Mandan to Consider Water for the Health of their Children, Grandchildren, and All Future Generations

relatedmaterial.head: Related Materials
relatedmaterial.p: See also: Inkjet Prints, 13 x 19 inches (32.8 x 48.2 centimeters). Mandan Courthouse , Box 6, folder 66

did.unittitle: North Dakota Highway 1806, Backwater Bridge, and Vicinity

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Photograph that documents an action along North Dakota Highway 1806, also known as ND 1806 and Route 1806 in Morton County, September-December 2016, to protest the Energy Transfer Partners project to construct a petroleum pipeline known as the Dakota Access Pipeline (DAPL). The image is a view of an activist.

arrangement.head: Arrangement
arrangement.p: Organized under one heading: Backwater Bridge.

title.part: Raising Awareness with Roadside Action, Route to Mandan

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Also known as Roadside Action and Roadside Action to Raise Awareness

relatedmaterial.head: Related Materials
relatedmaterial.p: See also: Inkjet Prints, 13 x 19 inches (32.8 x 48.2 centimeters). Roadside Action , Box 7, folder 86

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Photographs that document an action to occupy Turtle Island in the Cannonball River on Thanksgiving Day, November 24, 2016, to protest the Energy Transfer Partners project to construct a petroleum pipeline known as the Dakota Access Pipeline (DAPL). Includes images related to local authorities occupying the ridge of the island.

title.part: Turtle Island Action, Thanksgiving Day

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Also known as Thanksgiving Day, Turtle Island , and Turtle Island, Thanksgiving Day

relatedmaterial.head: Related Materials
relatedmaterial.p: See also: Inkjet Prints, 13 x 19 inches (32.8 x 48.2 centimeters). Thanksgiving Day, Turtle Island , Box 8, folder 94, and Turtle Island, Thanksgiving Day , Box 8, folder 98

title.part: Turtle Island Action, Thanksgiving Day

relatedmaterial.head: Related Materials
relatedmaterial.p: See also: Inkjet Prints, 13 x 19 inches (32.8 x 48.2 centimeters). Turtle Island Action, Thanksgiving Day , Box 8, folder 95

title.part: Turtle Island Action, Thanksgiving Day

relatedmaterial.head: Related Materials
relatedmaterial.p: See also: Inkjet Prints, 13 x 19 inches (32.8 x 48.2 centimeters). Turtle Island Action, Thanksgiving Day , Box 8, folder 97

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Photograph that documents an action that included United States Military Veterans at North Dakota, September-December 2016, to protest the Energy Transfer Partners project to construct a petroleum pipeline known as the Dakota Access Pipeline (DAPL).

title.part: Veterans Who Came to Stand for Water Protectors, Safeguarding them from Sheriff's Forces

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Also known as Veterans, Backwater Bridge and Veterans' Action on Backwater Bridge in Defense of Water Protectors #2

relatedmaterial.head: Related Materials
relatedmaterial.p: See also: Inkjet Prints, 13 x 19 inches (32.8 x 48.2 centimeters). Veterans, Backwater Bridge , Box 8, folder 106

arrangement.head: Arrangement
arrangement.p: Organized under four headings: Camp Overviews, Camps at Dawn, Camps During and After a Blizzard, and Camp Resources.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Photographs that document temporary camps, chiefly the Oceti Sakowin camp near Cannon Ball, North Dakota, and eastern edge of the Standing Rock Indian Reservation, established by activists protesting the Energy Transfer Partners project to construct a petroleum pipeline known as the Dakota Access Pipeline (DAPL), September-December 2016. Images include overviews of the camps, including views created at dawn or during and after a blizzard, as well as related to resources provided to inhabitants of the camps.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Also known as Portrait of Leonard Peltier, Oceti Sakowin Camp and A Painting of Leonard Peltier at Camp

relatedmaterial.p: See also: Inkjet Prints, 13 x 19 inches (32.8 x 48.2 centimeters). Portrait of Leonard Peltier, Oceti Sakowin Camp , Box 9, folder 115
